subject: Ārya
object: the nomadic half-savage Dāsas and Dasyus
verbatim_snippet: is the one word in the Rgveda that distinguished the agriculturists from the nomadic half-savage Dāsas and Dasyus who did not offer sacrifices to Indra
notes: Defines Ārya as agriculturists, distinct from nomads

subject: agriculture
verbatim_snippet: Since then, agriculture has ever been the chief industry of the country
notes: Claims agriculture's primacy in ancient India

subject: ancient Indian agriculture
verbatim_snippet: sacrifices were inaugurated in honour of Indra 'the wielder of the dreaded thunderbolt' the god who alone was believed capable of killing Vṛtra 'the demon of drought,' and thereby release the pent-up rains
notes: Describes religious practice for agricultural benefit

subject: rotation of crops
verbatim_snippet: the principle of rotation of crops was early known in India. Indeed, from the very Rgveda VIII, 91, 5-6, it appears that crops were grown in the same field by rotation.
notes: Defines rotation of crops as an ancient Indian practice

subject: tree planting
verbatim_snippet: It is best to plant trees at intervals of 20 cubits, next at 16, and 12 cubits interval is the minimum that can be prescribed-ed

subject: trees
object: too close or adjoining each other
conditions: otherwise, branches that touch bear no fruit
verbatim_snippet: they should not be planted too close or adjoining each other, in which case, the branches that touch one another should be lopped off with a chopper, as otherwise, they would bear no fruit.

subject: propagation by cuttings
verbatim_snippet: these are the plants to be propagated by means of cuttings besmeared with cow-dung

subject: grafting
object: inserting cuttings in root or stem
verbatim_snippet: the cuttings of one plant is either inserted in the root of another plant or on the stem of another plant

subject: grafts
verbatim_snippet: Grafts should be smeared with cow-dung.

subject: transplanting plants
object: ghee, sesame oil, honey, Vidanga, milk, cow-dung
verbatim_snippet: the plants should be smeared from root to the top with ghee (clarified butter), sesame oil, honey of the ksudra variety of bees of the Uśira (Andropogon Laniger or Andropogon Chitrarum), the Vidanga (Embelica ribes) milk and cow-dung

subject: sowing paddy
verbatim_snippet: Sowing in the month of Vaiśākha is best, in the month of Jyaistha is tolerable, Āsādha bad and Śrāvāna worst

subject: transplanting paddy
verbatim_snippet: Transplanting should best be done in Āsādha. It is bad to do it in Śravana and worst in Bhādra.

subject: second ploughing
verbatim_snippet: the field is ploughed for a second time in the month of Āṣāḍha or Śrāvaṇa, after the seeds grow into plants. This destroys some of them but they subsequently turn into manure and give the rest a healthier growth.
notes: Describes timing and effect of second ploughing

subject: hoeing after planting
conditions: if done in Śrāvana or Bhādra
verbatim_snippet: If hoeing is done in the month of Śrāvana or Bhādra, the harvest is doubled thereby, even if grass may again grow.
notes: Claims benefit of hoeing at specific times

subject: paddy field
verbatim_snippet: the water in the field is to be drained off in the month of Bhādra, leaving just enough for only the roots to be under water.

subject: harvesting
verbatim_snippet: In the month of Paus (after the Pusya-yātrā), the wise cultivator reaps full harvest

subject: ādhaka
predicate: is a unit of measure for grains
object: a vessel having a volume of 216 cu inches
verbatim_snippet: Here ādhaka is a unit of measure for grains. It is a vessel having a volume of 216 cu inches

subject: seasonable cultivation and harvesting
conditions: as per Kautilya and Taittiriya Samhitā
verbatim_snippet: These directions for seasonable cultivation and harvesting obviously point to the practice of rotation of crops in ancient India.
notes: States principle of rotation from ancient texts

subject: Atharvaveda
object: locusts, mice, borers and other devourers of corn
verbatim_snippet: The Atharvaveda mentions the locusts, the mice, the borers and other devourers of corn and gives an exorcism of these vermins in Hymn 50 of Book VI.
notes: Observes pest problems in ancient texts

subject: protection of crops
object: making a din and noise, use of scarecrows
verbatim_snippet: the cultivators kept away birds from the corn fields by making a din and noise, while in the Mahāvāgga, the use of scarecrows is mentioned.
